results.possibleIdentity: Ezra Peter Grossman (c. 1950–2026) was an engineer and activist who was active at the Columbia University School of Engineering in the late 1960s and early 1970s. During his senior year in 1970, he co-chaired the first Earth Day celebration at Columbia University and led his engineering class on strike in protest against the Vietnam War. His technical background included mathematics and computers, fields in which he remained active by tutoring students and applying quantitative skills in his early career. After graduating from Columbia, he worked for two years in New York City Mayor John Lindsay's policy planning department before attending Harvard Business School. He eventually had a long career in finance, working for commercial banks, investment firms, and Moody's Rating Agency, specializing in commercial real estate. He was a lifelong New Yorker, raised in the Bronx, and later lived in Greenwich, Connecticut, and Hendersonville, North Carolina.
